 {"id":"bd-26s","title":"Workstream: Comprehensive Test Coverage (No Mocks)","description":"# Workstream: Comprehensive Test Coverage (No Mocks)

## Goal
Achieve 95%+ test coverage using real implementations wherever possible, with
detailed logging for E2E integration tests. Eliminate reliance on mocks for
core functionality testing.

## Background
Current test coverage analysis reveals critical gaps:

### Well Tested (Good)
- Built-in Tools: 100% coverage (122 conformance fixture cases)
- Session Tree Logic: 11 tests covering branching/navigation
- SSE Parser: 9 tests covering all edge cases
- Auth Flow: 3 tests for PKCE/OAuth setup

### Critical Gaps (Bad)
- **Provider Streaming**: 0 tests with real API/streaming (only unit tests)
- **Interactive TUI**: 2 tests out of 2842 lines (<0.1%)
- **CLI/main.rs**: 0 tests for 1581 lines
- **Compaction**: 0 tests for 903 lines
- **Configuration**: 0 tests for 332 lines
- **Session Persistence**: Only serialization tested, not full I/O cycle

### What "No Mocks" Means
- Provider tests: Use VCR-style recorded responses, not fake providers
- File tests: Use real temp directories (already done)
- CLI tests: Use actual binary execution
- TUI tests: Use terminal emulator or snapshot testing
- Network tests: Use local test server or recorded responses

## Testing Strategy

### 1. Provider Streaming Tests (VCR Style)
Record real API responses and replay them:
- No external network calls during CI
- Real response parsing (not simplified mocks)
- Error scenarios from real API errors

### 2. E2E Integration Tests
Full workflow tests with verbose logging:
- Start pi binary with arguments
- Verify output and exit codes
- Check session files created
- Log every step for debugging

### 3. Snapshot Testing for TUI
Capture terminal output and compare:
- Render TUI components to string
- Compare against golden snapshots
- Update snapshots on intentional changes

### 4. Property-Based Testing
Use proptest for edge cases:
- Truncation with random inputs
- Session serialization round-trips
- Path resolution edge cases

### 5. Compaction Tests
- Window selection + summary insertion
- Tool/thinking/image content handling
- Session invariants preserved after compaction

### 6. Configuration Tests
- Config discovery + precedence rules
- Env + CLI override behavior
- Invalid config errors with actionable messages

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Provider streaming tested with recorded responses
- [ ] TUI logic tested (state transitions, rendering)
- [ ] CLI argument parsing fully tested
- [ ] Compaction logic tested
- [ ] Configuration loading tested
- [ ] Session persistence cycles tested
- [ ] E2E tests with verbose logging exist
- [ ] CI runs all tests in <5 minutes

## Files to Create
- tests/provider_streaming.rs - VCR-style provider tests
- tests/e2e_cli.rs - CLI integration tests
- tests/tui_snapshot.rs - TUI snapshot tests
- tests/compaction.rs - Compaction pipeline tests
- tests/config_loading.rs - Config discovery/override tests
- tests/fixtures/vcr/*.json - Recorded API responses

## Dependencies
None (can start immediately, independent of HTTP migration)","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:34:05.966405126Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T05:46:20.507382886Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-26s","depends_on_id":"bd-2qk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:34:05.973753960Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-9sa","title":"Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper","description":"# Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper

## Goal
Create a functional HTTP client in src/http/client.rs using asupersync APIs.

## Current State
src/http/client.rs has:
- Client struct (empty)
- RequestBuilder struct with method/url/headers/body
- RequestBuilder::send() returns Err("asupersync HTTP client not yet implemented")
- RequestBuilder::json() silently ignores serialization errors

## Implementation Requirements

### Client API
```rust
use asupersync::{Cx, http::client::HttpClient};
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnectorBuilder;

pub struct Client {
    inner: HttpClient,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Create TLS connector with native roots
        let tls = TlsConnectorBuilder::new()
            .with_native_roots()?
            .build()?;
        let inner = HttpClient::new(tls);
        Ok(Self { inner })
    }
    
    pub fn request(&self, method: Method, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder {
        RequestBuilder::new(self, method, url)
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
    pub fn post(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
}
```

### RequestBuilder API
```rust
impl RequestBuilder {
    pub fn header(mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn json<T: Serialize>(mut self, body: &T) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn body(mut self, bytes: Vec<u8>) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn timeout(mut self, duration: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub async fn send(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<Response, Error> {
        // Actually send the request using asupersync
    }
}
```

### Response API
```rust
pub struct Response {
    status: StatusCode,
    headers: Headers,
    body: BodyReader,  // Async streaming body
}

impl Response {
    pub fn status(&self) -> StatusCode { ... }
    pub fn headers(&self) -> &Headers { ... }
    pub async fn text(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<String, Error> { ... }
    pub async fn json<T: DeserializeOwned>(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<T, Error> { ... }
    pub fn bytes_stream(self) -> impl Stream<Item = Result<Bytes, Error>> { ... }
}
```

## Key Considerations
1. **TLS**: Use rustls with native root certificates (no openssl)
2. **Timeout**: Support per-request timeout via asupersync Budget
3. **Streaming**: Response body must support async streaming for SSE
4. **Headers**: Support standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
5. **Error handling**: Wrap asupersync errors in pi's Error type

## Testing
- Unit test: Client creation succeeds
- Unit test: RequestBuilder builds correct request
- Integration test: GET request to httpbin.org/get
- Integration test: POST with JSON body
- Integration test: Timeout behavior

## Files
- src/http/client.rs (modify)
- src/http/mod.rs (update re-exports)
- src/error.rs (add HTTP error variants)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Client::new() creates working HTTP client
- [ ] GET/POST requests work
- [ ] JSON body serialization works
- [ ] Response streaming works
- [ ] Timeout respected
- [ ] TLS verification works
- [ ] All unit tests pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:01.120130883Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:08.576779013Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:08.576706438Z","close_reason":"Completed (HTTP client wrapper is implemented + tests pass; see comments)","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-9sa","depends_on_id":"bd-gi3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:01.127860687Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":1,"issue_id":"bd-9sa","author":"AmberForest","text":"Taking this bead: implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper (no tokio/reqwest). Will wire streaming + TLS + timeout per spec.","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:45:33Z"},{"id":3,"issue_id":"bd-9sa","author":"AmberForest","text":"Implemented asupersync HTTP wrapper updates (Cx-aware send + timeout + StatusCode + bytes_stream). Migrated auth + npm registry calls to wrapper with Cx::for_request. Added http module re-exports.","created_at":"2026-02-03T04:05:06Z"},{"id":20,"issue_id":"bd-9sa","author":"CodexGPT52","text":"FYI: I just claimed bd-pwz (SSE streaming adapter). I’m going to unify src/http/sse.rs to re-export the canonical src/sse.rs implementation + add SseStream wrapper tests. Your bd-9sa implementation in src/http/client.rs looks already landed; after I run full quality gates I may propose closing bd-9sa to unblock provider-migration beads.","created_at":"2026-02-03T16:58:01Z"},{"id":26,"issue_id":"bd-9sa","author":"CodexGPT52","text":"Ran full quality gates on main workspace: cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo fmt --check, cargo test (all pass). src/http/client.rs is a working streaming HTTP+TLS client (providers/tests exercise it). Closing bd-9sa to unblock bd-pwz + provider migration beads; reopen if any missing API (e.g. explicit timeout/Cx) is still desired.","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:03Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-ah1","title":"Implement session persistence cycle tests","description":"# Implement session persistence cycle tests

## Goal
Test the full session lifecycle: create → save → reload → verify integrity, with detailed logs.

## Background
Session persistence involves:
- JSONL file format (one JSON object per line)
- Header with metadata (version, cwd, timestamp)
- Message entries with parent/child relationships
- Tree structure for conversation branching
- File locking for concurrent access

Current tests cover serialization but not full I/O cycles.

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- Use temp dirs and deterministic paths; avoid asserting exact timestamps.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log session path, header fields, message IDs, tree shape, and file sizes.
- On failure, dump the JSONL contents (redacted) and parsed header.

## Test Categories

### 1. Basic Persistence Cycle Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_session_save_and_reload() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project/path");
    session.add_user_message("Hello");
    session.add_assistant_message("Hi there!");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.messages().len(), 2);
}
```

### 2. Tree Structure Persistence Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_branching_persists() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project");
    let root_id = session.add_user_message("Root");
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch A");

    session.navigate_to(root_id.clone());
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch B");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    let children = loaded.children_of(&root_id);
    assert_eq!(children.len(), 2);
}
```

### 3. Header Metadata Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_header_metadata_preserved() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/my/project/path");
    session.set_provider("anthropic");
    session.set_model("claude-opus-4");
    session.set_thinking_level(ThinkingLevel::High);
    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.cwd(), "/my/project/path");
}
```

### 4. Entry Type Persistence Tests
- ModelChange entries
- ThinkingLevel entries
- Compaction entries
- Tool invocation entries (if serialized)

### 5. Error Handling Tests
- Missing file
- Corrupted JSONL
- Partial corruption (valid header + bad line)

### 6. Concurrent Access Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_concurrent_writes_locked() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let session1 = Session::new("/project");
    session1.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle1 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 1");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle2 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 2");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let (r1, r2) = join(handle1, handle2);
    assert!(r1.unwrap().is_ok() || r2.unwrap().is_ok());
}
```

### 7. Directory Structure Tests
- Persist creates nested directories when needed

## Dependencies
None (uses existing session module)

## Files
- tests/session_persistence.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 15+ session persistence tests
- [ ] Save/reload cycle tested
- [ ] Tree structure preserved
- [ ] All entry types tested
- [ ] Error handling tested
- [ ] Concurrent access tested
- [ ] Logs include paths + header fields
- [ ] All tests pass","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:00.745045518Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T07:33:57.212399249Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ah1","depends_on_id":"bd-26s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:00.753607302Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-ah1","depends_on_id":"bd-3ml","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T05:44:12.670095866Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-btq","title":"Extensions: Node/Bun-free runtime (PiJS) — prove superior path","description":"# Goal
Deliver a Node/Bun-free extension runtime that is **more secure, more deterministic, and faster** than legacy Pi’s Node/Bun-based model, while still running representative JS extensions **as-is** via a thin compatibility layer.

# Background / Motivation
- Mario noted that **QuickJS lacks the OS API wrapper and event loop** provided by Node/Bun, which legacy extensions rely on.
- We reject bundling a full Node/Bun runtime; instead we supply a **minimal, capability-gated PiJS runtime** with a deterministic event loop and focused OS connectors.

# Strategy (PiJS)
1) **Connector-first architecture** — all I/O is explicit `host.call` into the connector dispatcher; no ambient OS APIs.
2) **Minimal JS stdlib** — a small, auditable PiJS standard library (`pi.fs`, `pi.http`, `pi.timers`, etc.) that maps to connectors.
3) **Deterministic event loop** — microtasks, timers, and hostcall promises driven by asupersync with strict ordering rules.
4) **Compatibility layer** — extc pipeline rewrites Node/Bun imports to PiJS shims + rejects forbidden APIs with actionable diagnostics.
5) **Security & performance proof** — capability gating + resource limits + benchmarks that show parity or improvement.

# Success Criteria
- A representative JS extension runs without Node/Bun.
- Deterministic ordering across repeated runs.
- Capability policy enforced for every hostcall with structured logs.
- Benchmarks show runtime overhead within budgets (or better).
- Documentation + evidence binder make the approach self-contained and reproducible.

# Non-Goals
- Implementing a full Node/Bun compatibility layer.
- Allowing unrestricted FS/network access.

# Notes for Future Self
This issue is the umbrella for the **"prove them wrong"** path. All implementation tasks should preserve: explicit capabilities, deterministic scheduling, auditable logs, and minimal surface area. If you add a new API, update the compatibility matrix and include tests.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:18:20.201357583Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T17:18:20.201357583Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-doi","title":"Coverage tooling + CI gating (llvm-cov)","description":"Goal:
- Add repeatable coverage tooling (llvm-cov) and CI gating that enforces agreed coverage thresholds and the no-mock policy.

Why this matters:
- Coverage is only meaningful if it reflects real behavior (no mocks) and is stable in CI.
- We need artifacts + logs to debug coverage regressions quickly.

Scope:
- Choose tool (cargo llvm-cov preferred) and pin exact command(s).
- Add local + CI commands for coverage runs (text + optional HTML).

 {"id":"bd-gi3","title":"Workstream: HTTP Migration to asupersync","description":"# Workstream: HTTP Migration to asupersync

## Goal
Replace reqwest + tokio with asupersync HTTP client across all providers, enabling:
- Structured concurrency with cancel-correct operations
- Deterministic testing via LabRuntime
- Removal of tokio dependency (smaller binary, simpler runtime)
- Unified HTTP/TLS/SQLite stack from asupersync

## Background
asupersync is a sibling library at ../asupersync providing:
- Capability-based context (Cx) for async operations
- Built-in HTTP client with rustls TLS
- Structured cancellation (no orphaned futures)
- Deterministic LabRuntime for testing

Currently, pi_agent_rust has:
- src/http/mod.rs - Module stub
- src/http/client.rs - RequestBuilder that returns "not implemented" error
- src/http/sse.rs - SSE parser ready for integration
- All 4 providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Gemini, Azure) use reqwest directly

## Technical Approach
1. Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper in src/http/client.rs
2. Create SSE streaming adapter using existing src/http/sse.rs parser
3. Migrate Anthropic provider first (most complex, has extended thinking)
4. Migrate remaining providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Azure)
5. Remove reqwest + tokio dependencies from Cargo.toml
6. Update all async code to use Cx context

## Dependencies
- asupersync must compile (currently has 3 unused import warnings, otherwise OK)
- SSE parser (src/sse.rs) already exists and is well-tested (9 tests)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All HTTP requests go through asupersync client
- [ ] SSE streaming works for all providers
- [ ] tokio removed from Cargo.toml
- [ ] reqwest removed from Cargo.toml
- [ ] Tests pass with deterministic LabRuntime
- [ ] No performance regression (benchmark streaming throughput)

## Risks
- asupersync HTTP API may differ from reqwest
- TLS certificate handling may need attention
- Streaming may have subtle behavior differences

## Files Affected
- src/http/mod.rs
- src/http/client.rs
- src/http/sse.rs
- src/providers/anthropic.rs
- src/providers/openai.rs
- src/providers/gemini.rs
- src/providers/azure.rs
- Cargo.toml","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:31:41.845664872Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:31:41.852370167Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-gi3","depends_on_id":"bd-2qk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:31:41.852346893Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-ivj","title":"Migrate Azure OpenAI provider to asupersync HTTP","description":"# Migrate Azure OpenAI provider to asupersync HTTP

Replace reqwest usage in src/providers/azure.rs with asupersync HTTP client.

## Background
Azure OpenAI provider:
- Azure-hosted OpenAI API
- Custom endpoint URLs (resource.openai.azure.com)

1. **Endpoint**: Custom Azure resource URLs
2. **API Version**: ?api-version=2024-02-01 query param
3. **Authentication**: api-key header (not Bearer token)
4. **Compatible**: Same response format as OpenAI

## Dependencies
- bd-9sa, bd-pwz
- bd-1vo (OpenAI migration - nearly identical)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No reqwest imports
- [ ] 5 tests pass
- [ ] Streaming works with Azure endpoints","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:17.223664792Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:41.925483754Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ivj","depends_on_id":"bd-1vo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:41.925461983Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-ivj","depends_on_id":"bd-gi3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:33:17.233362232Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-pwz","title":"Implement SSE streaming adapter for asupersync","description":"# Implement SSE streaming adapter for asupersync

## Goal
Bridge asupersync HTTP streaming response to the existing SSE parser (src/sse.rs).

## Background
The SSE parser in src/sse.rs is already complete and well-tested:
- SseParser::new() creates parser
- SseParser::feed(&str) returns Vec<SseEvent>
- Handles: event types, data fields, IDs, comments, multiline data
- 9 unit tests covering all edge cases

The challenge is connecting asupersync's streaming response body to this parser.

## Implementation

### Streaming Adapter
```rust
// src/http/sse.rs (new file or extend existing)

use crate::sse::{SseParser, SseEvent};
use asupersync::Cx;

pub struct SseStream {
    parser: SseParser,
    body: BodyReader,
    buffer: String,
}

impl SseStream {
    pub fn new(response: Response) -> Self {
        Self {
            parser: SseParser::new(),
            body: response.into_body(),
            buffer: String::new(),
        }
    }
    
    pub async fn next(&mut self, cx: &Cx) -> Option<Result<SseEvent, Error>> {
        loop {
            // Try to parse buffered data first
            let events = self.parser.feed(&self.buffer);
            if !events.is_empty() {
                self.buffer.clear();
                return Some(Ok(events.remove(0)));
            }
            
            // Read more data from body stream
            match self.body.read_chunk(cx).await {
                Ok(Some(chunk)) => {
                    self.buffer.push_str(&String::from_utf8_lossy(&chunk));
                }
                Ok(None) => return None,  // Stream ended
                Err(e) => return Some(Err(e.into())),
            }
        }
    }
}

// Or implement as a proper Stream trait for use with async iteration
impl Stream for SseStream {
    type Item = Result<SseEvent, Error>;
    
    fn poll_next(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Option<Self::Item>> {
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Usage Pattern (in providers)
```rust
// In anthropic.rs
let response = client.post(url).json(&request).send(cx).await?;
let mut sse = SseStream::new(response);

while let Some(event) = sse.next(cx).await {
    match event? {
        SseEvent { data, event_type, .. } => {
            // Process Anthropic streaming event
        }
    }
}
```

## Considerations
1. **Backpressure**: Don't buffer entire response; process incrementally
2. **Error recovery**: Handle malformed events gracefully (log and continue)
3. **Cancellation**: Support clean shutdown via Cx cancellation
4. **Memory**: Limit buffer size to prevent OOM on malicious servers

## Dependencies
- bd-9sa (HTTP client wrapper must exist first)

## Testing
- Unit test: SseStream parses single event
- Unit test: SseStream handles multiline data
- Unit test: SseStream handles stream interruption
- Integration test: Stream from mock HTTP server

## Files
- src/http/sse.rs (new or extend)
- src/http/mod.rs (add re-export)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SseStream wraps Response and yields SseEvent
- [ ] Incremental parsing works (no full buffering)
- [ ] Cancellation handled cleanly
- [ ] Memory bounded
- [ ] All tests pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CodexGPT52","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:22.790834667Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:15.003720303Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:15.003659099Z","close_reason":"Completed (SSE parsing + streaming adapter in src/sse.rs; http::sse re-exports; tests added; all quality gates pass)","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-pwz","depends_on_id":"bd-9sa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:29.283945494Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-pwz","depends_on_id":"bd-gi3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:22.801694914Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":19,"issue_id":"bd-pwz","author":"CodexGPT52","text":"Claimed by CodexGPT52. Plan: (1) remove duplicated SSE impl in src/http/sse.rs by re-exporting the canonical crate::sse::{SseEvent,SseParser,SseStream} (better UTF-8 chunk handling + avoids tech debt), (2) add focused unit tests for SseStream wrapper behavior (UTF-8 split + multi-event chunks), (3) run cargo check/clippy/fmt/test, then close bd-pwz. Will coordinate with bd-9sa owner if any gating fixes are needed.","created_at":"2026-02-03T16:57:53Z"}]}
